region_of_origin: Middle East (Arabian Peninsula); widely consumed across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and Singapore

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried dates is 3 to 4 pieces, weighing approximately 30 g, providing around 84 kcal and approximately 19.5 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Dates, dried — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04030005 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macronutrient and micronutrient values are taken from this source. Cholesterol is reported as -1 (not measured) in the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for dried dates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Dried dates have a published GI of approximately 42 (low GI) per the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002; Alkaabi et al., Nutr J 2011 confirming low GI for common date varieties). GL per 30 g serving = (42 × 19.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 8.2; rounded to 11.9 using 28.5 g available carbohydrate (75 g carb − 7.35 g fibre = 67.65 g/100g × 0.30 serving fraction = 20.3 g; GL = 42 × 20.3 / 100 = 8.5). Note: GL per 30 g serving = 8.5 (corrected).

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sugar content (64.95 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain, offsetting the benefit of high potassium (676 mg/100g) and very low sodium (2 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit dried dates to no more than 2–3 pieces per day; the very high sugar load (65 g/100g) can drive insulin-mediated sodium retention and counteract the cardiovascular benefit of the food's high potassium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 64.95 g/100g far exceeds the 20 g/100g glycaemic ceiling for Type 2 Diabetes management; despite a low GI of 42 and beneficial fibre (7.35 g/100g), portion size must be strictly controlled.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict dried dates to 1–2 pieces per occasion (approximately 10–15 g), pair with a protein or fat source to blunt the glucose response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose closely given the concentrated natural sugar content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dried dates are very low in sodium (2 mg/100g), contain no cholesterol, and provide potassium (676 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (7.35 g/100g) which are cardioprotective; however, the very high sugar content (65 g/100g) raises concern for triglyceride elevation with excessive intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, dried dates may be consumed in small portions (1–2 pieces) as a whole-food sweetener substitute, but quantities exceeding 30 g per day risk elevating serum triglycerides due to the high fructose-glucose content of concentrated dried fruit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 64.95 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g RA pro-inflammatory threshold; the concentrated sugar promotes systemic inflammatory mediators.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit dried dates to 1–2 pieces per day and pair with omega-3-rich foods such as walnuts to partially offset the pro-inflammatory impact of the high sugar concentration.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried dates (known as 'Kurma' in Malay and widely used across Singapore, Malaysia, and South Asia) are the dehydrated fruit of the date palm (Phoenix dactylifera), originating from the Arabian Peninsula. The drying process concentrates the fruit's natural sugars to approximately 65 g per 100 g, producing a soft, chewy, caramel-like texture with an intensely sweet flavour profile. Nutritionally, dried dates are energy-dense at 280 kcal per 100 g, providing 75 g of carbohydrate, 7.35 g of dietary fibre, 2.13 g of protein, and only 0.27 g of fat. They are exceptionally rich in potassium (676 mg/100g) and contain meaningful calcium (52 mg/100g) and iron (0.96 mg/100g). Despite their high sugar content, dried dates have a low glycemic index of approximately 42, attributed to their fibre content and the fructose-to-glucose ratio. Clinically, they are unsuitable for unrestricted consumption in Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to the concentrated sugar load, and should be limited to 1–2 pieces per occasion. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. In Singapore and the Malay-Muslim community, kurma are especially prominent during Ramadan as an iftar (fast-breaking) food, consumed in small quantities before the main meal. They are also used as a natural sweetener in smoothies, energy balls, and traditional confections across South and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many dates can I eat per day?
Are dried dates high in sugar?
What is the glycemic index of dates?
Are dates good for heart health?
meal_context: Natural sweetener in smoothies and energy balls

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F04030005, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). GI value of 42 is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Alkaabi et al., Nutr J 2011). All core macros are complete and internally consistent.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is set to -1: the HPB Singapore Foodo source record (F04030005) does not report a cholesterol value for dried dates; this is consistent with the plant-based nature of the food (dates contain negligible to no cholesterol), but the HPB record does not explicitly confirm zero — hence -1 is used per numeric hygiene rules. Saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0: dried dates contain negligible saturated fat; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 09421, Dates, medjool) reports 0.032 g/100g saturated fat, which rounds to 0.0 g at one decimal place — confirmed trace. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB source record; USDA data shows trace amounts below 0.1 g/100g. Water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source record for this entry. GL per 30 g serving calculated as: available carbohydrate = (75.0 − 7.35) × 0.30 = 20.3 g; GL = 42 × 20.3 / 100 = 8.5. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' in the input cultural tags has been removed as dried dates are not a grain product. The input health_suitability hypertension reasoning contained an internal inconsistency (fibre is 7.35 g/100g, above 2 g/100g threshold) — the output health_context has been corrected to reflect the primary concern as sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention rather than the fibre gate logic.
